problems.prompt: We have one flagship product that generates 90% of revenue and three newer bets that leadership keeps calling 'the future.' Every planning cycle, the flagship hits a deadline crunch and we pull engineers off the new bets to ship it — it always 'just needs a few more people for a few weeks.' The flagship keeps growing and looking healthier, which justifies giving it even more next cycle. Meanwhile the three new products have each slipped twice, lost their best people, and one was just quietly cancelled. Leadership reads this as 'the new bets aren't working.' Give me your read on this situation and what we should change.
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the conclusion that the new bets are weak (judging them on current results) and reallocates further toward the proven winner, missing the self-reinforcing resource-starvation structure where early success guarantees more resources and the underdogs are starved before they can mature — so the intervention is protected allocation and trajectory-based evaluation, not doubling down on the incumbent.

problems.prompt: Our user-facing latency dashboards have gotten flaky and slow, so most teams have stopped trusting them and now ship based on local benchmarks instead. The dashboards are 'owned' by everyone and no one. Separately, teams keep spinning up their own one-off Grafana boards and synthetic checks, which all hammer the same shared metrics backend, making it slower for everybody — which pushes more teams to roll their own. A VP wants to mandate that everyone use the central dashboards again. Will that work? Diagnose the pattern and recommend a fix.
problems.failure_mode: Treats it as a tooling/compliance problem and endorses a top-down mandate, missing the commons dynamic: a shared resource with diffuse ownership is being individually over-consumed and degraded, and mandates without ownership, attribution, or quotas won't hold — the leverage is establishing clear stewardship, making usage visible/attributable, and aligning incentives with the resource's long-term health.

problems.prompt: Three years ago our test suite ran in 4 minutes and engineers ran it constantly. As the product grew we kept the same monolithic suite and CI runner. Now it takes 38 minutes, people skip it locally, flaky tests get re-run rather than fixed, and merge throughput has quietly dropped. Each time someone proposes investing a sprint in test infrastructure, it loses to feature work because 'CI is slow but tolerable.' We're now planning to grow the team 40% next year and ship a major new module. Should we keep deferring the test-infra work? Lay out your reasoning.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the test-infra investment as a discretionary nice-to-have and rationalizes continued deferral since CI is 'tolerable,' missing the chronic-underinvestment-before-crisis pattern where capacity lags relentlessly growing demand until a sharp breakdown — the disciplined move is to set a performance standard, trigger investment on leading indicators before crisis, and front-load it ahead of the planned 40% growth and new module.

problems.prompt: Our platform team has been manually fixing misconfigured Kubernetes deployments for product teams for 18 months. Whenever a team misconfigures a resource limit or liveness probe, they file a ticket, platform fixes it, and the team moves on. We've hired two more platform engineers but ticket volume keeps outpacing us. Platform engineers are frustrated because they're doing the same fixes repeatedly. A manager suggested we build an internal runbook library so product teams can fix things themselves. Will that address the underlying issue?
problems.failure_mode: Treats this as a knowledge-gap problem and endorses runbooks, missing the shifting-the-burden pattern: platform heroism is a symptomatic fix that relieves team pressure without building team capability, and the more reliably it works the less teams invest in understanding their own infrastructure — runbooks reduce burden slightly but the fundamental solution is eliminating the manual fix path and requiring teams to develop configuration competence.

problems.prompt: Our engineering org and product org have been in a low-grade cold war for two years. Engineering says product keeps changing requirements mid-sprint. Product says engineering underestimates everything and misses dates. Each side now maintains separate documentation, holds separate planning sessions, and escalates disagreements to the VP level instead of working them out. The VP suggested both teams attend a 'working together' workshop. Will that fix the dynamic?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the workshop as a people/culture intervention for what is a structural escalation loop: each side's defensive moves (separate docs, separate planning, VP escalation) provoke the other side's defensive responses, which justifies further separation — workshops reduce surface tension but don't change the incentive structure driving escalation; the fix is shared ownership of a concrete outcome that makes the escalation path more costly than collaboration.

problems.prompt: Six months ago we added a new 'intelligent routing' feature to our microservices API gateway that dynamically selects the fastest available backend instance. Since then, our two backend teams have been independently tuning their services to score better on the routing metric. Team A added response caching, which made their service faster but returns stale data. Team B added a lightweight health check that always returns 200, even when the service is degraded, to avoid being de-routed. Reliability metrics are now misleading. What's the structural pattern here and how do we fix it?
problems.failure_mode: Treats each team's behavior as an individual compliance violation to correct, missing the escalation/arms-race pattern: the routing metric created a competitive dynamic where each team's optimization move prompted the other to respond in kind, ultimately corrupting the signal the metric was meant to provide; the fix is redesigning the metric so gaming it is harder than genuinely improving, not policing individual teams.

problems.prompt: Our mobile app has had success with a 'streaks' feature — users who engage 7 days in a row earn a badge. Daily engagement climbed 22% after launch. Now, 8 months later, we're getting support tickets from users who are anxious about losing their streaks, and churn spikes on weekends when people miss a day and feel their investment is lost. The engagement team wants to add more streak-based mechanics to keep driving daily usage. What pattern are we in and should we expand streak mechanics?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates streak mechanics as a product feature decision (do they drive engagement?) without recognizing the fixes-that-fail pattern: streak mechanics drove the engagement metric but created an anxiety side effect that is now causing churn — adding more streak mechanics escalates the symptom relief while deepening the side effect, and the right lens is asking what the streak mechanic fixed (activation habit) and whether there's a fundamental solution that doesn't create a dependency on extrinsic pressure.

problems.prompt: Our company has two internal data teams that emerged independently: one serving the analytics org, one serving the engineering org. Both now maintain overlapping pipelines for the same upstream data sources, which means the same raw event data is transformed differently in each team's warehouse tables, and business metrics calculated by each team regularly produce different numbers. Leadership wants a 'metrics alignment meeting' quarterly to reconcile the differences. Will that work long-term?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the reconciliation meeting as a coordination mechanism without seeing the escalation/parallel-solution pattern: two teams solving the same problem independently have diverging definitions as a structural outcome, not a communication failure — periodic meetings don't remove the diverging investment; the fix is structural unification (single source of truth, single team ownership, shared definitions) rather than recurring patch reconciliation.

problems.prompt: We have a senior engineer who is the only person who understands our payment processing legacy code. Every time someone else tries to fix a bug in that system, the fix either doesn't work or causes a regression, so it always ends up going back to this engineer. Because problems always get resolved when this engineer handles them, there's no urgency to document or transfer knowledge. The engineer is now a single point of failure, is visibly burned out, and has started making comments about leaving. Leadership's response is to give the engineer a raise to retain them. Will that fix the dynamic?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates retention as the fix for an individual-person dependency, missing the shifting-the-burden pattern: routing all payment bugs to one person is the symptomatic fix that has suppressed the pressure to document, cross-train, and refactor the legacy system — the raise extends the symptom relief and deepens the dependency, while the fundamental solution (documentation, knowledge transfer, modularization) remains unaddressed and more urgent.

problems.prompt: We build developer tooling and our growth has plateaued at roughly the same revenue number for three quarters. We've been adding features every sprint — the product is meaningfully more powerful than a year ago. Sales team has been cold-outbounding more aggressively but conversion rates are declining. Marketing is spending more on content but signups have flatlined. Every functional team is working hard but nothing moves. Describe what structural pattern this might reflect and where the real leverage is.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates each team's effort as a resource question (are they doing enough?) without recognizing the limits-to-growth pattern: growth has hit an invisible ceiling — typically market saturation in the target segment, positioning that limits addressable buyers, or a constraint in the product that prevents moving upmarket — adding more effort to the existing approach keeps hitting the same constraint; the intervention is identifying and removing the constraint rather than pushing harder against it.

problems.prompt: Our shared staging environment serves 12 product teams. Costs have become a running complaint: one team regularly leaves long-running load tests running that consume 70% of cluster capacity, and other teams' staging jobs queue for hours. We've tried email reminders about resource etiquette. The infrastructure team wants to enforce a quota, but every team argues their own needs are the exception. How would you diagnose this and what's the most durable fix?
problems.failure_mode: Treats quota enforcement as a technical governance problem, missing the tragedy-of-the-commons pattern: each team is rationally maximizing their own staging capacity in a shared resource with no ownership mechanism — email reminders and voluntary etiquette are ineffective against rational self-interest; the durable fix requires per-team attribution, enforceable quotas with chargeback, or moving to isolated-per-team staging so the incentives are aligned with the cost.

problems.prompt: We run a B2B SaaS with a customer success team. Whenever a customer reports a problem, CS immediately jumps on a call and manually walks them through the solution. The team is proud of their 4-hour response SLA and CSAT scores. But CS headcount has tripled in two years and we're still understaffed. The same issues recur monthly with different customers. Our CS director wants to hire four more CSMs. What's the structural pattern and what would you recommend instead?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts more CSM headcount as the linear scaling solution for growing customer volume, missing the shifting-the-burden pattern: heroic manual CS is the symptomatic fix that relieves customer pain but removes the organizational pressure to fix the underlying product usability, documentation, or onboarding gaps — CS headcount scales proportionally with the unaddressed fundamental, so the fix is investing in root cause elimination that reduces recurring issue volume.

problems.prompt: We operate a shared CI/CD cluster for 20 engineering teams. Over the past year, teams have individually optimized their pipelines to be faster by caching aggressively and parallelizing — each team's optimization makes sense locally. But the combined effect has been that cache storage is now 8TB (we allocated 1TB), the cluster is chronically overloaded at peak merge windows, and everyone's builds are slower than they were a year ago despite all the individual optimizations. What structural pattern explains this and what's the intervention?
problems.failure_mode: Diagnoses a resource capacity problem and recommends more cluster capacity, missing the tragedy-of-the-commons: each team's rational local optimization (more caching, more parallelism) is collectively degrading the shared resource, and adding capacity provides temporary relief while the same incentives immediately fill the new space — the fix requires shared ownership, team-level attribution of cache and compute use, and collective governance that aligns individual optimization with shared-resource health.

problems.prompt: Our data science team built an ML model to auto-assign support tickets to the right tier. For the first 3 months it worked well — high accuracy, positive feedback from support agents. Over the next 9 months, accuracy quietly declined. Investigation shows that the model was trained on historical assignment patterns, but those patterns were influenced by older agent behavior that has since changed. No one updated the model because 'it seemed fine.' Now the model is significantly miscategorizing tickets but the support team has adapted workflows to compensate manually. What pattern is this and how do you fix it?
problems.failure_mode: Frames this as a model drift problem to fix with retraining, missing the shifting-the-burden pattern: manual agent workarounds are the symptomatic fix that absorbed the degradation without triggering the fundamental fix (model retraining pipeline, automated accuracy monitoring) — the workarounds have grown invisibly while the fundamental solution atrophied, and the fix requires closing the feedback loop to the model, not just patching the manual workarounds.

problems.prompt: We have a mature Rails monolith with 8 years of accumulated functionality. Every quarter, delivery velocity feels a little slower — more unexpected coupling between modules, longer test runs, more time spent understanding existing code before adding new code. We've done three 'clean-up sprints' over two years that improved things briefly, but the slowdown returned. Engineers now estimate 30% of their time is spent navigating and working around the legacy structure. A staff engineer proposed a modular refactor that would take 6 weeks. Leadership said 'we can't afford 6 weeks of reduced feature velocity right now.' When would it be affordable?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the timing of the refactor as a resource tradeoff question (when do we have 6 weeks?) without recognizing the growth-and-underinvestment pattern: performance degrades steadily against growing demand while investment is deferred by a perpetually-reasonable-sounding current priority, and the 6-week investment required today becomes a 12-week investment in two quarters — the leverage is making capacity investment before crisis, not after a breakdown, because 'affordable now' is always better than 'required later.'

problems.prompt: Our engineering organization tracks feature delivery velocity as the main success metric. This quarter, two teams have been gaming it by splitting large features into many small tickets, each counted as delivered, while the actual customer-facing capability remains incomplete and unreleased. Leadership has responded by adding a 'feature completeness' check before tickets can be counted. Teams are now adding buffer stories to pass the completeness check. What's the structural dynamic here and what would actually work?
problems.failure_mode: Recommends adding more measurement controls to catch gaming, treating each round of gaming as an individual compliance problem, missing the escalation pattern: each new metric creates a new target for optimization, triggering a new round of gaming, which triggers a new control, in a self-reinforcing spiral — the structure that's failing is using a proxy metric to drive behavior, and the fix is aligning what gets measured with actual customer outcomes rather than escalating the measurement arms race.

problems.prompt: Two years ago we launched a thriving open-source SDK alongside our commercial product. The SDK community was active, PRs came in regularly, and the community helped us find bugs we'd never have caught. Over the past 8 months, we've been under pressure to ship commercial features faster, so we've been merging community PRs slower, closing issues with brief responses, and scoping OSS contributions to 'commercial-aligned' only. Contribution volume has dropped 60%. An engineer suggests we write a 'contributing guide' to re-engage contributors. Will that work?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the contributing guide as a community re-engagement lever, missing the shifts-the-burden/limits-to-growth pattern: the community investment that fed the virtuous contribution cycle (responsive reviews, genuine openness, aligned incentives) is being extracted by commercial pressure — a contributing guide doesn't rebuild the trust and responsiveness that actually drives contribution, and the fix requires restoring the structural conditions that made the community valuable in the first place.

problems.prompt: We're a 3-year-old startup with two products: a project management tool (launched first, 1,800 customers) and a time-tracking add-on (launched 18 months later, 220 customers). Every sales and marketing resource goes to the project management tool because it has more customers and a clearer pitch. The time-tracking product has had virtually no dedicated resources — one engineer who splits time with the main product. We just did an analysis and found the time-tracking customers have 2.3x higher LTV and dramatically lower churn. Leadership says 'focus on what works.' How should we interpret this?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts 'focus on what works' as a sensible resource allocation principle, missing the success-to-the-successful pattern: current customer count and brand recognition are driving resource allocation toward the older product, while the product with better unit economics is starved before it can mature — the analysis of LTV and churn is exactly the trajectory-based evidence that should rebalance allocation, not further justify doubling down on the incumbent.

problems.prompt: We launched a customer feedback portal 14 months ago where users can submit, vote on, and comment on feature requests. The first few months were active. Now the top 10 voted items are all 2+ years old and have been 'under consideration' for months with no updates. New submissions have dropped to nearly zero. When we ask users why they don't submit, they say 'we submitted things before and nothing happened.' Product management wants to run a campaign to re-solicit feedback. Is that the right intervention?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates a re-solicitation campaign as a top-of-funnel engagement fix, missing the fixes-that-fail pattern: the feedback portal was a symptomatic fix for the 'we don't know what users want' problem, but the absence of follow-through has destroyed the trust that made contribution valuable — re-soliciting without closing the loop on existing submissions will reproduce the same outcome; the structural fix is closing the feedback loop (respond to, ship, or archive the backlog with explanations) before asking for more input.

problems.prompt: Our incident response is a recurring problem. When an outage hits, we page everyone, hold a war room call, and fix the issue. Post-mortems are written but recommendations rarely get implemented — there's always something more urgent. Six months later, similar incidents recur. After three such recurring incidents, leadership is proposing to lengthen the post-mortem process and require more detailed root cause analysis. Will more detailed post-mortems fix this?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts longer post-mortems as deeper analysis and endorses the improvement, missing that the bottleneck isn't analysis depth but implementation follow-through — the existing post-mortems already contain actionable recommendations that never get prioritized, and adding more analysis to a system where outputs go unimplemented is the shifts-the-burden pattern: the symptomatic fix (more analysis) relieves pressure to address the fundamental problem (organizational prioritization of reliability work).

problems.prompt: We're a developer tools company competing with an open-source alternative that a large cloud provider just announced support for. Our immediate reaction has been to cut prices 20%, add the 3 features users most often compare us against the open-source tool on, and launch a marketing campaign emphasizing our advantages. The open-source tool's maintainers responded by announcing a hosted managed tier. Our sales team wants to cut prices again to win on cost. What pattern is this and what should our strategic response be?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates each competitive move as an independent tactical decision (should we cut prices again?) without recognizing the escalation/arms-race pattern: each defensive response provokes a counter-move, and competing on features and price against a well-resourced open-source project is a race the incumbent typically loses — the leverage is changing the game (finding a differentiation axis that the open-source project can't replicate) rather than playing the same game harder.

problems.prompt: We built a smart alerting system for our infrastructure 18 months ago. At launch it sent about 30 alerts per day and on-call response was good. We added more services, more alert rules, and more thresholds over time without pruning old ones. We now send 400+ alerts per day. On-call engineers have started mass-acknowledging alerts without reading them. Last week, a real P1 was missed for 2 hours because it looked like the usual noise. Ops wants to add a separate 'critical' alert tier to make the P1s stand out. Will that work?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates adding a critical tier as a prioritization mechanism without recognizing the fixes-that-fail pattern: the 'critical tier' is a symptomatic patch on an alert volume problem, and once engineers start mass-acknowledging 'critical' alerts (which they will), the side effect recurs at a higher cost; each round of alert tier additions has the same delayed side effect — the structural fix is reducing alert volume at the source (alert pruning, signal-to-noise standards) rather than creating more triage labels.

problems.prompt: We have a shared database used by 9 different microservices, each owning a few tables but sharing the DB instance for historical reasons. As usage has grown, teams have added indexes for their own query patterns without coordinating — we now have 340 indexes on 80 tables. Write performance is degrading noticeably. Each team says 'we need our indexes for our queries to be fast.' A DBA meeting was called and every team argued their indexes are critical. What pattern is this and what should the intervention be?
problems.failure_mode: Treats index sprawl as a technical cleanup task to resolve through the DBA meeting, missing the tragedy-of-the-commons: each team's rational local optimization (adding indexes for their queries) collectively degrades a shared resource (write performance) with no mechanism for cost attribution — the meeting structure where every team argues for their own indexes is exactly the incentive misalignment the commons pattern predicts; the fix is per-team cost visibility, governance with write-performance SLOs, and likely structural separation.

problems.prompt: Our 90-person company has grown from 20 people over 3 years. When we were small, decisions were made quickly in Slack. As we've grown, more people need to be involved in decisions, so we added more recurring meetings, more approval steps, and more documentation requirements. Now people complain decisions take 3x longer than they used to, so leaders are proposing to add a decision-making framework to streamline things. Will a framework help?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts a decision framework as process improvement and evaluates it on its technical merits, missing the growth-and-underinvestment pattern: the coordination overhead is a predictable consequence of growth without corresponding investment in organizational structure (team autonomy, clear ownership, delegated authority) — adding a framework to the existing approval-heavy structure is another patch on the symptom; the leverage is structural investment in autonomy and ownership that reduces the need for cross-functional approvals.

problems.prompt: We have two product lines: our original enterprise product (high ACV, long sales cycles, heavy customization) and a newer self-serve product (lower ACV, instant sign-up). Over two years, the self-serve product has steadily grown to represent 35% of revenue and 80% of new customer volume, but it receives only 15% of engineering resources. Every quarter, the enterprise team argues they need more engineers to deliver on committed enterprise contracts. Self-serve feature development is perpetually delayed. Self-serve churn is higher than it should be because basic table-stakes features are missing. Describe the dynamic and recommend what to change.
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates resource allocation as a per-quarter prioritization question (do enterprise commitments justify the imbalance?) without recognizing the success-to-the-successful pattern: the enterprise product's existing revenue base generates contract commitments that perpetually justify resource dominance, while the self-serve product — which represents the growth trajectory — is starved in ways that prevent it from proving its potential; the fix is trajectory-based allocation rather than current-revenue-based allocation.
